summary: Jeff Atwood responds to critics who claimed Stack Overflow could be cloned in a weekend by arguing that while the code itself is relatively trivial, the real work lies in the user experience and everything surrounding the code. He quotes Benjamin Pollack's insight that developers only see database schemas when looking at products, missing the tremendous polish that makes sites successful. Atwood admits Stack Overflow's source code is indeed 'kind of trivial' but emphasizes that his team's mission is creating the best experience, not the best code. He lists thirteen non-coding activities that actually drive Stack Overflow's success, from community moderation to podcasting to advertising. The post concludes that writing code is trivial and fun, but success requires stopping to do all the other 'trivial stuff' around the code.
key_points: Developers underestimate project complexity because they only see the database schema and basic HTML, not the polish that makes products successful
Stack Overflow's code is admittedly trivial, but the team's mission is to create the best experience, not the best code
Most of what makes Stack Overflow successful has nothing to do with writing code—it's community management, support, content, and business operations
Open source clones will eventually approximate commercial products, but motivated closed-source teams can outrun them for a while
Success requires stopping coding long enough to do all the other necessary work around the code
money_quotes: When you put a developer in front of StackOverflow, they don't really see StackOverflow.
A developer, asked how hard something will be to clone, simply does not think about the polish, because the polish is incidental to the implementation.
Despite Benjamin's well reasoned protests, the source code to Stack Overflow is, in fact, actually, kind of... well, trivial.
Not to write the best Stack Overflow code possible, but to create the best Stack Overflow experience possible.
We try very, very hard not to suck.
There's nothing particularly magical about the production of source code.

Writing code is trivial. And fun. And something I continue to love doing. But if you really want your code to be successful, you'll stop coding long enough to do all that other, even more trivial stuff around the code that's necessary to make it successful.
key_insight: The code behind successful products is often trivial; what makes them succeed is the relentless focus on user experience and all the non-coding work that developers tend to dismiss as unimportant.
